subject: ap-csp
theme: Algorithms and programs use sequencing, selection, iteration, procedures, and lists to solve problems — and understanding their efficiency, equivalence, and limits determines what computing can and cannot do.

core_concepts.title: Sequencing, Selection, and Iteration
core_concepts.detail: The three building blocks of all algorithms are sequencing (statements execute in order), selection (IF/ELSE chooses between paths based on a condition), and iteration (REPEAT loops execute code multiple times). Every program is a combination of these three elements.

core_concepts.title: Variables, Lists, and Assignment
core_concepts.detail: Variables store values that can be read and reassigned during execution. The CB pseudocode assignment operator is ← (not =). Lists store multiple values under one name, accessed by 1-based index — list[1] is the first element.

core_concepts.title: Procedures and Procedural Abstraction
core_concepts.detail: Procedures (functions) group reusable code under a name, accept parameters, and optionally return a value. Procedural abstraction means callers use a procedure without knowing how it is implemented — hiding complexity behind a clean interface.

core_concepts.title: Data Abstraction and Libraries
core_concepts.detail: Data abstraction uses lists (and other structures) to manage multiple related values as a single unit — instead of 20 separate score variables, use scores[1] through scores[20]. Libraries provide pre-written procedures that programmers can call without writing them from scratch.

core_concepts.title: Linear Search vs. Binary Search
core_concepts.detail: Linear search checks every element one by one (works on any list). Binary search repeatedly halves the search space by comparing to the middle element — it requires a sorted list but is dramatically faster for large datasets (log₂N steps vs N steps).

core_concepts.title: Algorithm Efficiency and Limits
core_concepts.detail: Algorithm efficiency matters at scale: an algorithm that does n² operations for an input of size n becomes impossibly slow as n grows large. Some problems cannot be solved in reasonable time regardless of hardware; others (undecidable problems) cannot be solved by any algorithm.

core_concepts.title: Code Equivalence and Simulations
core_concepts.detail: Two code segments are equivalent if they produce identical outputs for every possible input — not just for specific test cases. Simulations model real-world processes computationally, allowing scientists to test scenarios that would be impractical in the real world.

exam_tip: The AP exam heavily tests tracing CB pseudocode — practice reading code with nested conditionals, REPEAT UNTIL loops, and list operations. Binary search requires a sorted list and takes at most log₂(n) steps; the Halting Problem is the canonical undecidable problem.
